questions.specification:
A long-term analysis of U.S. population data was conducted using the *National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES)* from 1988 to 2015. The study evaluated glycaemic control using $HbA_{1c}$ levels — a biomarker that reflects average blood glucose over 2–3 months.

$HbA_{1c}$ is a critical indicator of glucose homeostasis and is used both in diagnosing and monitoring diabetes. The prevalence of $HbA_{1c}$ categories was analyzed across different age groups and between sexes for individuals with and without diabetes. Additionally, the study assessed hazard ratios (risk of adverse health outcomes such as cardiovascular disease or mortality) in relation to $HbA_{1c}$ levels, both in those with diagnosed diabetes and without.

Three figures summarize the findings:

Figure 1: Prevalence of $HbA_{1c}$ levels by sex and age group in individuals diagnosed with diabetes.

![](https://142c8bdb1fea8b57b0fb24ca54327b99.eu.r2.cloudflarestorage.com/user-question-part-images/iHw-MzyuRkEI3KlJQ64vr)

Figure 2: Prevalence of $HbA_{1c}$ levels by sex and age group in individuals without diabetes.

![](https://142c8bdb1fea8b57b0fb24ca54327b99.eu.r2.cloudflarestorage.com/user-question-part-images/oy6L53jTpNkvCH1ySgLHV)

Figure 3: Hazard ratios (with $95\%$ confidence intervals) by $HbA_{1c}$ group, comparing individuals with diagnosed diabetes vs. those without. $HbA_{1c}$ $5.0\text{--}5.6\%$ in non-diabetics is used as the reference group.

![](https://142c8bdb1fea8b57b0fb24ca54327b99.eu.r2.cloudflarestorage.com/user-question-part-images/kqbHMeMvgO4rb67hfL_Uq)

parts.markscheme:

parts.content: Explain how the nervous system maintains blood pressure during standing.
parts.markscheme:
- On standing, venous pooling in the lower limbs reduces venous return and stroke volume, causing a fall in arterial blood pressure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Baroreceptors detect the reduced stretch/pressure and signal the medulla oblongata <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The medulla increases sympathetic output and reduces parasympathetic output, increasing heart rate (and contractility) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sympathetic activity causes vasoconstriction in arterioles/veins, increasing peripheral resistance and venous return to restore blood pressure <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how the endocrine and nervous systems coordinate to regulate blood pressure during an acute fight-or-flight response.
parts.markscheme:
- Sympathetic nervous system stimulates the adrenal medulla to release adrenaline into the bloodstream <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Adrenaline increases heart rate and myocardial contractility, increasing cardiac output <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sympathetic output causes vasoconstriction in non-essential vascular beds (e.g. splanchnic), increasing total peripheral resistance and redistributing blood flow <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Adrenaline supports/augments sympathetic effects (e.g. acting on adrenergic receptors) to maintain/increase arterial pressure during stress <marks>1 mark</marks>
- These coordinated actions help maintain cerebral perfusion and reduce risk of dizziness/syncope <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the importance of inter-system communication in maintaining stable blood pressure during dynamic exercise (e.g. cycling).
parts.markscheme:
- Autonomic nervous system increases heart rate and contractility to raise cardiac output during exercise <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sympathetic control of vessel tone redistributes blood flow (vasodilation in active muscle with vasoconstriction in some non-essential beds) to support performance while maintaining pressure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Endocrine responses (e.g. adrenaline) help sustain cardiovascular adjustments and support blood pressure during ongoing activity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Coordination with the respiratory system increases ventilation to meet increased oxygen demand and support aerobic metabolism <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Feedback/feedforward control (e.g. baroreflex resetting and reflexes from working muscle) helps stabilise perfusion and aid waste removal during exercise <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Maintaining arterial pressure is important for adequate delivery of oxygen/nutrients to working muscles and continued cerebral perfusion <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: When blood $\text{CO}_2$ levels rise during exercise, how does the body maintain homeostasis?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.2 Maintaining homeostasis > A.1.2.1 Mechanisms and processes maintaining homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2.1 Mechanisms and processes maintaining homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Rising blood CO2 during exercise triggers negative feedback mechanisms to maintain homeostasis (ventilation adjustments to regulate pH/CO2).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.3 Transport > A.1.3.2 The respiratory system and gas exchange for cellular respiration
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.3.2 The respiratory system and gas exchange for cellular respiration
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The specific effector response is increased respiratory rate/ventilation to remove excess CO2 via the respiratory system and maintain gas exchange.

options.content: The respiratory rate decreases to conserve energy
options.markscheme: Incorrect. A rise in blood $\text{CO}_2$ (and $\text{H}^+$) is detected by chemoreceptors, which stimulates an increase in ventilation to remove $\text{CO}_2$, not a decrease.

options.content: The kidneys increase urine production
options.markscheme: Incorrect. The kidneys are primarily involved in osmoregulation and the excretion of nitrogenous waste, not the immediate regulation of blood $\text{CO}_2$ levels.

options.content: The respiratory rate increases
options.markscheme: Correct. Rising blood $\text{CO}_2$ levels lead to a decrease in blood pH. This is detected by chemoreceptors in the medulla oblongata and blood vessels, which stimulate an increase in the ventilation rate to expel $\text{CO}_2$.

options.content: The heart rate increases to remove $\text{CO}_2$ from the blood
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Increasing heart rate can increase transport of $\text{CO}_2$ to the lungs, but the primary homeostatic response to elevated blood $\text{CO}_2$ is increased ventilation (respiratory rate/depth) to exhale more $\text{CO}_2$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.2 Maintaining homeostasis > A.1.2.1 Mechanisms and processes maintaining homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2.1 Mechanisms and processes maintaining homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which homeostatic response restores acid–base balance after sprinting-induced pH decrease, focusing on negative-feedback regulation of blood pH.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.3 Transport > A.1.3.2 The respiratory system and gas exchange for cellular respiration
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.3.2 The respiratory system and gas exchange for cellular respiration
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The correct response involves changing ventilation to alter CO2 removal via the respiratory system, directly linking gas exchange/ventilation to blood pH regulation.

options.content: Decreased respiratory rate to slow bicarbonate loss
options.markscheme: A decreased respiratory rate would cause $\text{CO}_2$ to accumulate further, resulting in a more significant drop in pH and worsening the acidosis.

options.content: Increased ventilation rate to expel $\text{CO}_2$ and raise pH
options.markscheme: Rising levels of $\text{CO}_2$ during exercise lower blood pH. Chemoreceptors detect this change and trigger an increase in ventilation rate to expel $\text{CO}_2$, which drives the bicarbonate buffer system to consume $H^+$ ions and restore pH balance.

options.content: Sympathetic inhibition to reduce oxygen demand
options.markscheme: During exercise, the sympathetic nervous system is activated, not inhibited, to increase heart rate and ventilation to meet higher metabolic demands.

options.content: Increased renal $H^+$ excretion and bicarbonate reabsorption
options.markscheme: The kidneys can help correct acidosis by increasing $H^+$ excretion and reabsorbing bicarbonate, but this response is slower and is less likely to be the primary immediate homeostatic adjustment during a short, intense sprint compared with increased ventilation.

options.markscheme: Correct: Increased $\text{CO}_2$ entering the red blood cell drives $\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightleftharpoons \text{HCO}_3^- + \text{H}^+$ to the right, directly increasing $\text{H}^+$ (and $\text{HCO}_3^-$) formation.

options.content: Increased hemoglobin buffering capacity reduces plasma $\text{pH}$
options.markscheme: Incorrect: Hemoglobin buffering helps bind $\text{H}^+$ and resist a fall in pH; it does not directly reduce plasma pH as a consequence of $\text{CO}_2$ entering the red blood cell.

options.content: Increased $\text{H}^+$ release leads to a decrease in blood $\text{pH}$ and stimulates ventilation
options.markscheme: Incorrect for this stem: Although increased $\text{CO}_2$ can contribute to lower blood pH and stimulate ventilation via chemoreceptors, this is a downstream systemic response rather than the direct intracellular consequence of $\text{CO}_2$ entry into the red blood cell.

options.content: Carbonic anhydrase converts bicarbonate into carbon dioxide to increase $\text{pH}$
options.markscheme: Incorrect: In systemic tissues, carbonic anhydrase mainly catalyzes $\text{CO}_2$ hydration to form $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$ (leading to $\text{HCO}_3^-$ and $\text{H}^+$). The reverse direction is favored in the lungs.

A healthy individual consumes a glucose-rich drink at $t = 0$. Their blood glucose levels are monitored over the following 90 minutes, as shown in the table below.

| Time (minutes) | Blood glucose concentration ($\text{mg dL}^{-1}$) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 80 |
| 30 | 145 |
| 60 | 115 |
| 90 | 90 |

Which mechanism is primarily responsible for the trend observed between 30 and 90 minutes?

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2.1 Mechanisms and processes maintaining homeostasis

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1.2 The endocrine system and regulation of biological processes

options.content: Glucagon stimulates the liver to convert stored glycogen into free glucose.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Glucagon primarily increases blood glucose and is not the main mechanism for the decrease from 30 to 90 minutes.

options.content: Insulin promotes the uptake of glucose by body cells, reducing blood glucose concentration.
options.markscheme: Correct. After the rise in blood glucose, insulin release increases glucose uptake (and storage as glycogen), lowering blood glucose between 30 and 90 minutes.

options.content: A positive feedback loop is initiated to amplify the metabolic rate of all body tissues.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Blood glucose regulation operates mainly via negative feedback, not positive feedback, and does not globally amplify metabolic rate as the primary mechanism.

options.content: The hypothalamus signals the adrenal glands to release cortisol to stabilize energy levels.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Cortisol is a longer-term stress hormone that tends to increase blood glucose; it is not the primary mechanism for the postprandial decrease shown.

questions.specification:
A multidisciplinary study investigated how occupation and lifestyle affect Non-Exercise Activity Thermogenesis (NEAT) — the portion of daily energy expenditure that excludes sleeping, eating, and sports-like exercise. NEAT is influenced heavily by occupational demands such as sitting, standing, or engaging in manual labor.

One part of the study compared NEAT (in $\text{kcal/day}$) for individuals with different occupational movement demands, assuming a constant basal metabolic rate (BMR) of $1600\text{ kcal/day}$. Another part of the study analyzed work-related activity durations across age groups and between men and women in agricultural regions of the Ivory Coast. Finally, researchers examined the components of Total Energy Expenditure (TEE), highlighting how NEAT contributes to overall energy output and why some individuals are more responsive to changes in physical activity than others.

Figure 1: NEAT levels by occupation type

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/EJduOH-1LuGDIwl7MciXS)

Figure 2: Work duration (min/day) by age and gender

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/mtcpPYakFEaRZqrTpuCbT)

Figure 3: Components of TEE, including BMR, thermic effect of food, and activity-related energy expenditure

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/cOPkpsxSNXX7zffLsG8iA)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.2 Benefits to health of being active > A.3.2.1 Active lifestyle and physical well-being
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.2.1 Active lifestyle and physical well-being
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question centers on NEAT as a major contributor to daily energy expenditure and how occupational/lifestyle activity levels affect health and sedentary behavior, aligning with active lifestyle and physical well-being.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.2 Fuelling for health and performance > A.2.2.1 Macronutrients and their role in energy maintenance
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.2.1 Macronutrients and their role in energy maintenance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses kcal/day, BMR, TEE components (including thermic effect of food) and asks how NEAT affects total energy expenditure—core nutrition/energy balance concepts linked to macronutrients/energy maintenance.

parts.content:
Using Figure 1, state how occupational movement influences NEAT. State the approximate NEAT value for:
- Chair-bound individuals
- Individuals performing strenuous agricultural work
parts.markscheme:
- Occupational movement shows a positive correlation with NEAT levels (as movement demands increase, NEAT increases)
- Chair-bound individuals: $\approx 300\text{ kcal/day}$
- Strenuous work (agriculture): $\approx 2300\text{--}2400\text{ kcal/day}$
<marks>A1</marks>

<Note>Award 1 mark for all correct components. Accept ranges $\pm 100\text{ kcal}$.</Note>

parts.content: Calculate the difference in NEAT between seated work (no option to move) and standing work.
parts.markscheme:
- Seated work (no option to move): $\approx 700\text{ kcal/day}$
- Standing work: $\approx 1400\text{ kcal/day}$
- Difference: $\approx 700\text{ kcal/day}$
<marks>A1</marks>

<Note>Award 1 mark for correct value $\pm 100\text{ kcal}$.</Note>

parts.content: Explain how occupation-related NEAT differences could affect daily total energy expenditure, even in individuals with the same body size and age.
parts.markscheme:
- Individuals with similar age, sex, and body mass may have large differences in total energy expenditure due to the high variability of NEAT between different job types <marks>R1</marks>
- For example, two adults with similar BMRs could differ by up to $\approx 1500\text{ kcal/day}$ based solely on their occupational demands (e.g., sedentary office work vs. active manual labor) <marks>A1</marks>

<Note>Award 1 mark for identifying the impact of NEAT and 1 mark for linking it to daily energy expenditure via specific magnitude/comparison.</Note>

parts.content: Refer to Figure 2. Identify the age group in which women spend the most minutes working per day.
parts.markscheme: Women aged 25–29 years show the highest daily work duration ($\approx 470\text{ minutes}$) <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Compare the work duration of men and women in the 25–29 age group.
parts.markscheme:
- Men: $\approx 330\text{ minutes/day}$
- Women: $\approx 470\text{ minutes/day}$
- Difference: Women work $\approx 140\text{ minutes}$ more per day than men <marks>A1</marks>

<Note>Award 1 mark for identifying the large gap or providing both correct values.</Note>

parts.content: Describe one sociocultural or physiological reason for the gender gap in work-related physical activity across the working-age groups.
parts.markscheme:
- Sociocultural explanation: In subsistence agricultural societies, women may take on both domestic labor and field labor roles, increasing total work duration <marks>R1</marks>
- Physiological explanation: There are no inherent physiological limitations preventing women from engaging in long-duration activity; the gap reflects differences in lifestyle/societal expectation rather than physical capacity <marks>A1</marks>

<Note>Award 1 mark for a valid point and 1 mark for the corresponding explanation.</Note>

parts.content: Using Figure 3, identify which component of TEE remains most constant, regardless of activity levels.
parts.markscheme:
Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR) <marks>A1</marks>

<Note>Accept descriptions stating BMR contributes the majority ($\approx 60\text{--}70\%$) of TEE regardless of activity level.</Note>

parts.content: Explain why NEAT may increase more in habitually sedentary individuals than in those who are already highly active.
parts.markscheme:
- Sedentary individuals have lower baseline NEAT, meaning changes in daily movement lead to larger proportional increases in energy expenditure <marks>R1</marks>
- Highly active individuals may reach a "ceiling effect" where they have already maximized incidental movement, leaving less scope for further NEAT increases <marks>R1</marks>

<Note>Award 1 mark for contrast between responders vs. active and 1 mark for mechanism/justification.</Note>

parts.content: Explain how occupational NEAT could be incorporated into public health strategies to address sedentary behavior in modern industrialized societies. Support your answer with evidence from all three figures.
parts.markscheme:
- Public health focus should shift toward incidental movement/NEAT rather than only formal gym-based exercise, as BMR remains constant and NEAT is the most modifiable component (Figure 3) <marks>R1</marks>
- Workplace interventions can target specific job types by converting "seated (no option)" roles into standing or mobile roles, potentially doubling occupational calorie burn (Figure 1) <marks>A1</marks>
- Strategies should address demographic disparities, targeting age cohorts or genders with lower work-related activity to ensure equitable health outcomes (Figure 2) <marks>A1</marks>
- Implementing standing desks or active breaks can provide the $\approx 700\text{--}1500\text{ kcal/day}$ increase necessary to combat obesity without requiring significant time for sport (Figures 1 and 3) <marks>A1</marks>

<Note>Award marks for well-reasoned application of data from job type (Fig 1), demographics (Fig 2), and TEE components (Fig 3).</Note>

options.content: Faster breathing to remove $\text{CO}_2$
options.markscheme: Correct: increased ventilation removes $\text{CO}_2$, reducing carbonic acid and helping raise blood pH.

options.content: Slower breathing to retain $\text{CO}_2$
options.markscheme: Incorrect: retaining $\text{CO}_2$ increases carbonic acid, which would further lower blood pH.

options.content: Increased renal excretion of $\text{H}^+$ and reabsorption of $\text{HCO}_3^-$
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this does help correct acidosis but is a slower response than the immediate ventilatory compensation typically emphasized during acute exercise.

options.content: Reduced bicarbonate buffering in the blood
options.markscheme: Incorrect: reducing buffering would worsen the fall in pH rather than restore homeostasis.
